cataloging_subject.label: Joseph of Arimathie
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The anonymous Middle English alliterative poem conventionally titled Joseph of Arimathie, surviving acephalously but apparently complete at the end in the Vernon Manuscript, Bodleian Library MS. Eng. poet. a. 1, ff. 403ra–404vb. The subject is the poem represented by 709 extant edited and encoded line positions, not Joseph of Arimathea as a historical or legendary person, the whole Vernon codex, Walter W. Skeat's four-text 1871 volume, its three appended Joseph pieces, the separately bound Legends of the Holy Rood, the University of Michigan encoding and page images, or the checked-in repository XML.

catalog_summary.title: Joseph of Arimathie
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous Middle English poet
catalog_summary.editor: Walter W. Skeat, working from George Parker's transcript
catalog_summary.region: England; West Midlands associations, with witness scribal language localized to Worcestershire

titles.supplied_by: Walter W. Skeat and modern cataloging convention

assertion.notes: The sole manuscript has no surviving opening title; this is a conventional editorial title.

titles.value: The Romance of the Seint Graal, or Holy Grail

assertion.notes: This is Skeat's editorial subtitle, not a surviving manuscript heading.

titles.value: … sire he seis · and sonenday is nouwe

scope.description: Defective opening of the sole surviving witness

assertion.notes: The initial ellipsis records lost text rather than a complete work incipit.

titles.value: And þei lenden of þe toun · and leuen hit þere

assertion.notes: Skeat concludes that nothing appears lost at the end.

agents.name: George Parker
notes: Skeat identifies him as an assistant in the Bodleian Library.

assertion.notes: The U-M inherited catalog field naming Joseph of Arimathea as AUTHOR describes the poem's subject and is not evidence of authorship.

scope.description: Working transcript of the Vernon poem prepared for the 1871 edition

notes: Skeat revised Parker's transcript, divided the continuously written manuscript text into long lines, punctuated and hyphenated it, and expanded contractions.

scope.description: Careful revision of Parker's working transcript

assertion.notes: Carley's modern survey uses c. 1375. Skeat's 1871 linguistic and metrical argument instead preferred about 1350 and no later than 1360; neither approximation is normalized to an exact year.

date_statements.display: Sole surviving witness copied c. 1390–1400, with a contextual lower bound of 1381

assertion.notes: This dates the codex and attestation, not the poem's composition.

scope.description: First printing of the surviving poem

notes: No exact place of composition is established.

assertion.notes: Skeat argues that the poem was originally West Midland and was southernized by the Vernon scribe; this is a dialect claim, not proof of composition place.

scope.description: Scribal profile of the sole Vernon witness

assertion.notes: DIMEV links Vernon to LALME/eLALME LP 7630 and Worcestershire. This localizes witness language, not the anonymous poet.

form_statements.description: An unrhymed alliterative long-line poem in Skeat's edition and current DIMEV classification. The Vernon scribe wrote it continuously like prose, using capitals, paraphs, and metrical points to signal divisions. Modern scholarship retains the verse classification while debating whether the surviving text is a formally incomplete draft awaiting alliterative revision.

assertion.notes: Carley summarizes Lagorio's and Noble's readings of the adaptation as a hagiographic and didactic saint's life focused on Joseph's evangelizing role.

subject_statements.label: Evalak/Mordrains and the kingdom of Sarras
The White Knight and Evalak's shield

summaries.value: The surviving poem begins after Joseph of Arimathea's forty-two-year imprisonment. Joseph, his son Josaphe, and their company journey to Sarras with Christ's blood; Joseph teaches the Trinity and seeks to convert King Evalak. Evalak's war with Tholomer, the miraculous White Knight, healings, baptisms, and the renaming of Evalak and Seraphe as Mordrains and Naciens occupy the later narrative. The poem ends after Josaphe and Naciens depart on a mission and a brief proleptic account of their imprisonment and rescue.

Only the poem on Skeat's printed pp. 1–23 is cataloged. His prose Lyfe, De sancto Joseph, verse Lyfe, notes, glossaries, indexes, and the separately bound Legends of the Holy Rood remain outside the intellectual-work boundary even though they occur in, or are bibliographically associated with, the same 1871 volume.

The manuscript and current catalogs agree that the opening is defective. Skeat estimated that fewer than one hundred lines were lost and that the original may have had about eight hundred lines, but these are historical estimates, not a recoverable normalized extent. The 709 count describes extant editorial and encoded line positions. Skeat found no loss at the end, so the record preserves the asymmetric boundary: acephalous, apparently complete at end.

The poem's Christian conversion narrative includes polemical representations of Jews and Muslims, mass baptism, punitive violence, and warfare. These features are documented for responsible discovery and interpretation, not endorsed as historical truth.

extent.description: One surviving acephalous verse text represented by 709 nonblank BODY L elements. The 709 positions correspond exactly to Skeat's lineation: 172 explicit N attributes mark the expected fourth-line intervals and special positions through line 709, with no positional mismatch. The XML's 34 LG elements vary from 4 to 51 lines and are encoder containers labeled stanza, not a defensible claim that the intellectual work has 34 stanzas. The full encoded carrier has four DIV1 containers—two TYPE='omitted front matter' placeholders, the single BODY TYPE='text' container, and one TYPE='omitted back matter' placeholder—plus a retained TITLEPAGE and 148 PB elements linking page-image scaffolding. Those title-page, placeholder, and PB structures describe the digitized 1871 carrier rather than additional sections or items of the poem, so they are audited here but excluded from numbered_items, prose/verse item counts, verse groups, and verse lines; sections is deliberately null because no intrinsic section count is asserted. Eight manuscript milestones map Skeat's older side-and-column notation from 403a through 404b:2; the current manuscript locus is ff. 403ra–404vb. The witness itself presents the text continuously like prose. The unknown lost opening is not assigned a numeric line count, while the extant endpoint is treated as complete.

content_parts.label: Surviving acephalous poem
content_parts.summary: The 709 extant alliterative long-line positions, beginning mid-sentence with Joseph's release already under discussion and ending intact after the mission of Josaphe and Naciens is announced.

locators.value: ff. 403ra–404vb; f. 402 is missing before the defective opening; the poem ends in column 404vb before the following Life of Pilate and Judas begins on the same folio and column
canvases 684–687 of 710; fols. 403r–404v; the end canvas is shared with the following Pilate/Judas item

notes: The 34 encoded LG containers are retained as representation metrics and are not treated as intrinsic stanzas.
The shared f. 404v/column-b boundary does not include any part of the following South English Legendary Life of Pilate and Judas in Joseph.

related_works.title: Estoire del saint Graal
notes: Skeat and later scholarship identify the early part of the Old French Vulgate Cycle's Estoire as the principal source. The Middle English poem is a heavily abbreviated adaptation with changes, not a witness to one identified French manuscript exemplar.

related_works.title: Queste del saint Graal
notes: Carley reports Lawton's suggestion that the poet also used the Queste but judges the textual authority slight; no direct source relation is asserted.

related_works.title: South English Legendary: Life of Pilate and Judas
notes: The adjacent codex item begins at f. 404vb, on the same canvas and column where Joseph ends. It is not part of Joseph.

evidence.citation: General Corpus pinned CME source, source/CME_phase_1-2/aha2700.xml at commit e1d77c88ca561be924b2fedc54dd474be2e7e49c.
evidence.locator: complete file; BODY text; header and title page
evidence.summary: Exact evidence for AHA2700 identifiers, the 2006 uncorrected-OCR/TEI Level 1 statement, Skeat title page, 148 page milestones, 709 encoded poem lines, printed pages 1–23, manuscript milestones, and omitted-front/back boundaries.

evidence.citation: Target-scoped XML structure and lineation audit of the pinned aha2700 source.
evidence.locator: HEADER, FRONT, BODY, and BACK element census; all 709 BODY L elements
evidence.summary: Found four DIV1 elements, 709 nonblank BODY L elements, 34 variably sized LG containers, 148 PB elements, eight manuscript milestones, and no verbal text outside the poem and title page. All 172 explicit line-number attributes match positional line numbers through 709. Printed poem pages are 1–23; no printed p. 24 poem text is encoded.
notes: The L count measures encoded editorial positions, not physical manuscript lines or a reconstructed complete work.

evidence.citation: Walter W. Skeat, ed., Joseph of Arimathie, Early English Text Society, Original Series 44 (London: N. Trübner, 1871).
evidence.locator: preface pp. vii–xvi, especially vii–ix; poem pp. 1–23 = PDF pages 55–77
evidence.summary: Primary evidence for the four-piece volume boundary, missing f. 402 and defective opening, Parker's working transcript, Skeat's revision and lineation practices, the 709 extant lines, apparently intact ending, historical c. 1350 dating and West Midland dialect argument, narrative contents, and derivation from the Estoire del saint Graal.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library, Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse, AHA2700.0001.001.
evidence.summary: Current official item page for the encoded poem, lineation, item title, stable identifier, publication statement, and U-M public-domain/No Copyright - United States assertion.
notes: The inherited AUTHOR field naming Joseph of Arimathea is rejected as an authorship statement because the poem is anonymous.

evidence.citation: Bodleian Libraries, Catalogue of Western Medieval Manuscripts, MS. Eng. poet. a. 1, item 363; TEI source pinned at medieval-mss commit cd36dce9a104617f960dab2c97ea78f95f917496.
evidence.locator: msItem 363, ff. 403ra–404vb; adjacent msItem 364 begins f. 404vb; codex origin and physical description
evidence.summary: Current official evidence for the defective Joseph item, exact locus, incipit, explicit, Middle English language, adjacent Pilate/Judas boundary, c. 1390–1400 codex date with 1381 lower bound, English origin, physical extent, and missing f. 402.

evidence.citation: Digital Bodleian complete IIIF facsimile and structure ranges for the Vernon Manuscript.
evidence.locator: canvases 684–687 / bundle members images/000684.jpg–images/000687.jpg / ff. 403r–404v; Joseph range LOG_0371; following Pilate/Judas range LOG_0372
evidence.summary: Visual and structural inspection confirms the mid-sentence opening, continuous-prose manuscript presentation, four target surfaces, and shared f. 404v end canvas: Joseph ends in column b before the following Life of Pilate and Judas begins on the same folio and column.

evidence.summary: Versioned current census evidence for DIMEV 4864 / IMEV-NIMEV 3117.4, 709 alliterative lines, saint's-life subjects, defective opening, prose manuscript presentation, Skeat pp. 1–23, one recorded Vernon witness at ff. 403ra–404vb, and the witness's LALME/eLALME LP 7630 Worcestershire profile.
notes: Version 2.0-alpha.1 is a prerelease; the one-witness statement is scoped to this exact current census.

evidence.citation: James P. Carley, ‘England,’ in Norris J. Lacy, ed., Medieval Arthurian Literature: A Guide to Recent Research (New York and London: Garland, 1996), 1–82, at 19–20.
evidence.locator: chapter pp. 19–20; PDF pages 39–40 of the 49-page publisher preview
evidence.summary: Modern scholarly synthesis for c. 1375 dating, survival only in Vernon, continuous-prose presentation, Skeat's alliterative lineation, derivation from the Estoire, Lawton's debated draft-form theory, the weakly supported Queste proposal, and hagiographic/didactic readings of the adaptation.

open_questions.question: How much text and which exact narrative material preceded the defective opening in the lost f. 402 portion?
next_steps: Consult David Lawton's 1983 critical edition and later codicological studies for any refinement of Skeat's estimate.
Keep the opening extent unknown unless physical or textual evidence supports a number.
notes: Skeat's fewer-than-one-hundred-line loss and approximately 800-line original are historical estimates, not normalized facts.

open_questions.question: How should the poem's irregular alliterative form and continuous-prose manuscript presentation be described in light of Lawton's first-draft theory and its reviewers?
next_steps: Consult Lawton 1983 and the reviews summarized by Carley before adding a more granular metrical classification.
Retain verse/alliterative discovery terms while keeping manuscript layout and formal debate explicit.

notes: DIMEV 2.0-alpha.1 records one witness; unique-witness wording is therefore scoped to that exact current census.
All work metadata keeps the medieval work, physical witness, Parker transcript, Skeat edition, U-M encoding, repository artifact, and modern facsimiles as separate layers.
Rights and access for those layers are documented separately in the linked lineage manifest.
